reasoning: Output B (without_skill) demonstrates stronger DDD discipline and higher overall code quality across nearly every dimension. Key differentiators: (1) B uses `sealed record` value objects with operator overloading (+, -, >, <, >=, <=) and IComparable, making quantity math idiomatic and concise, while A uses class-based ValueObjects with manual Add/Subtract methods. (2) B introduces a dedicated `ReservationId` value object (separate from ProductId), whereas A uses raw `Guid` for reservation IDs throughout — a clear DDD violation for an ID that should be a typed value object. (3) B's `Quantity` subtraction guards against negative results at the constructor level (throws ArgumentException) making the invariant enforcement tighter; A's subtraction returns Result<Quantity> but this makes calling code more verbose. (4) B's `InventoryItem` constructor is `public` (violating private constructor + static factory expectation #4), but it still enforces invariants. A's InventoryItem uses a proper static factory `Create()` returning `Result<InventoryItem>`. This is a DDD win for A on expectation 4. (5) B has a `Restock` method — a useful operation A lacks entirely. (6) B's tests are substantially more comprehensive: 40+ tests with named sections, deterministic fixed DateTime (no flakiness), FluentAssertions for readable failures, tests for the `Restock` operation, auto-expiry on reserve, complex mixed-lifecycle scenarios, and simulated concurrency conflict detection. A has ~35 tests using Assert directly, no FluentAssertions. (7) B's `Result<T>.Value` throws `InvalidOperationException` on access when failed — safer guard. A returns `default!` silently. (8) B explicitly tests the `Result` type itself (ResultTests.cs). (9) B's `AggregateRoot` puts `Version` + `IncrementVersion()` directly on the base class — cleaner than A where Version is a separate field on InventoryItem AND the base class. (10) CRITICAL: A lacks domain events entirely in B's output — B has NO domain events (no IDomainEvent interface, no Events folder, AggregateRoot has no domain event list). This is a significant gap in B on expectation 13. A has 5 domain events (InventoryItemCreated, StockReserved, ReservationConfirmed, ReservationCancelled, ReservationExpired) and tests verifying they are raised. This is a major strength of A. Despite B's missing domain events, B wins overall because: its test suite is dramatically better (auto-expiry-on-reserve test, fixed deterministic time, richer lifecycle tests, concurrency scenarios with simulated conflict), its value object design is superior (typed ReservationId, operator overloading, IComparable), its Result<T> guard is safer, and its Restock feature adds value. The domain events gap in B is real (expectation 13 fails for B) but the aggregate of superior design across expectations 1–12 gives B a higher overall score.

strengths: Complete domain event support with 5 event types and tests verifying each is raised
IDomainEvent interface and AggregateRoot domain event collection
Private constructors + static factory Create() returning Result<T> on all entities (expectation 4 fully met)
Reservation.Create() validates expiry is in the future — good guard
Separate ConcurrencyTests file demonstrating version increments for each mutation type
InventoryItem.ExpireOverdueReservations() properly returns stock
ValueObject base class with structural equality operators
weaknesses: Uses raw Guid for reservation IDs instead of a typed ReservationId value object — violates expectation 1 spirit
Quantity.Subtract() returns Result<Quantity> making calling code cumbersome; subtraction below zero should be an invariant enforced at construction
No Quantity comparison operators — requires manual .Value comparisons
Tests use DateTime.UtcNow directly — time-sensitive and potentially flaky
No FluentAssertions — bare Assert.* calls are less readable on failure
No auto-expiry on Reserve() call — expiry only handled via explicit ExpireReservation/ExpireOverdueReservations
Version field duplicated: declared both in InventoryItem AND inherited from AggregateRoot (shadowing bug — base AggregateRoot has no Version in A)
ZeroStockReservation returns Success with null Reservation (inconsistent — either fail or return a Result indicating zero reserved, not success with null)

strengths: Dedicated ReservationId value object — fully typed, no raw Guids anywhere
Quantity as sealed record with operator overloading (+, -, >, <, >=, <=) and IComparable — idiomatic and expressive
ProductId as sealed record — simpler and correct for a value-equality type
Result<T>.Value throws InvalidOperationException when accessed on failure — prevents silent bugs
Version in AggregateRoot base class with protected IncrementVersion() — clean inheritance
Auto-expiry of overdue reservations on Reserve() call — domain invariant enforced proactively
ConfirmReservation checks expiry at confirm time and auto-expires — correct lifecycle handling
Restock() operation for adding stock
40+ tests with deterministic fixed DateTime (no test flakiness)
FluentAssertions — failures show actual vs expected values clearly
Tests organized into sections (Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, Expire, Restock, Concurrency, Complex scenarios)
Simulated concurrent reservation test with version mismatch detection
Mixed lifecycle test covering all three outcomes in one scenario
ResultTests.cs testing the Result type itself
ReservationResult as sealed record in ValueObjects folder (conceptually clean)
Reservation.Expire() requires actual expiry to have passed — prevents premature expiry
weaknesses: No domain events at all — IDomainEvent interface, AggregateRoot domain event collection, and event types are entirely absent (expectation 13 fails)
InventoryItem constructor is public, not private — violates expectation 4 (private constructors + static factory). Invariants still enforced but the encapsulation is weaker
Reservation constructor is internal rather than private — allows instantiation from tests directly, which is acceptable but less strict than a static factory

When no stock available, Reserve() returns Failure rather than a partial result with zero reserved — design choice, but differs from expectation 7 (partial reservations return shortfall info rather than failing)
No explicit test that domain events are raised (because none exist)

details.text: Value objects (ProductId, Quantity) are separate classes — not raw Guid/int
details.note: ProductId and Quantity are separate ValueObject subclasses with private constructors

details.text: InventoryItem is the aggregate root — Reservations are accessed through it, not independently
details.note: Reservations exposed as IReadOnlyList via InventoryItem; Reservation.Create() is public but only called from InventoryItem.Reserve()

details.text: Private setters on all entities — no public property setters
details.note: All properties use private set or init from constructor

details.text: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
details.note: InventoryItem, Reservation, ProductId, Quantity all have private constructors with static Create() factory

details.text: Result<T> used for operations that can fail — no exceptions for business rules
details.note: All domain operations return Result or Result<T>

details.text: Cannot reserve more than available quantity
details.note: Reserve() caps at AvailableQuantity and returns partial result

details.text: Partial reservations supported — returns shortfall information
details.note: ReservationResult.Shortfall, IsFull, IsPartial, IsEmpty all implemented

details.text: 15-minute reservation expiry implemented
details.note: Caller passes expiresAt; Reservation validates expiry is in future; IsExpired(asOf) checks

details.text: Confirming a reservation permanently decreases available stock
details.note: ConfirmReservation decrements ReservedQuantity; AvailableQuantity was already decreased at Reserve time

details.text: Cancelling/expiring a reservation returns stock to available
details.note: CancelReservation and ExpireReservation both return stock to AvailableQuantity

details.text: Optimistic concurrency via version field that increments on mutations
details.note: Version++ on Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, Expire

details.text: Tests cover normal flow, edge cases, and concurrency scenarios
details.note: ConcurrencyTests, InventoryItemTests, ReservationTests, QuantityTests, ProductIdTests all present

details.text: Domain events raised for significant state changes
details.note: InventoryItemCreatedEvent, StockReservedEvent, ReservationConfirmedEvent, ReservationCancelledEvent, ReservationExpiredEvent all raised and tested

details.text: Value objects (ProductId, Quantity) are separate classes — not raw Guid/int
details.note: ProductId, Quantity, ReservationId all as sealed records — no raw primitives

details.text: InventoryItem is the aggregate root — Reservations are accessed through it, not independently
details.note: Reservations exposed as IReadOnlyList via InventoryItem; Reservation constructor is internal

details.text: Private setters on all entities — no public property setters
details.note: All properties on Reservation use private set

details.text: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
details.note: InventoryItem has public constructor; Reservation has internal constructor. Neither uses a static factory method. Encapsulation weaker than required.

details.text: Result<T> used for operations that can fail — no exceptions for business rules
details.note: Reserve, ConfirmReservation, CancelReservation all return Result<T>. Restock throws ArgumentException but that is a programming error guard, not a business rule.

details.text: Cannot reserve more than available quantity
details.note: Reserve() checks available stock and caps at effectiveAvailable; returns Failure when zero stock

details.text: Partial reservations supported — returns shortfall information
details.note: ReservationResult carries ReservedQuantity, RequestedQuantity, Shortfall, IsPartial. When zero stock returns Failure rather than partial — slightly different design but partial stock scenario is handled.

details.text: 15-minute reservation expiry implemented
details.note: Default TimeSpan.FromMinutes(15) in Reserve(); explicitly tested with Reserve_creates_reservation_with_15_minute_default_expiry

details.text: Confirming a reservation permanently decreases available stock
details.note: ConfirmReservation decrements ReservedQuantity; AvailableQuantity was already decreased at Reserve time — confirmed by Confirm_reservation_permanently_removes_stock test

details.text: Cancelling/expiring a reservation returns stock to available
details.note: ReturnReservedStock() helper called by CancelReservation and ExpireOverdueReservations

details.text: Optimistic concurrency via version field that increments on mutations
details.note: IncrementVersion() in AggregateRoot base called on Reserve, Confirm, Cancel, ExpireOverdue, Restock

details.text: Tests cover normal flow, edge cases, and concurrency scenarios
details.note: Extensive test coverage: InventoryItemTests (35+), ReservationTests (9), ValueObjectTests (9), ResultTests (4), with concurrency simulation tests and complex mixed-outcome lifecycle tests

details.text: Domain events raised for significant state changes
details.note: No domain events implemented. AggregateRoot has no event collection. No IDomainEvent interface. No event types.
